denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Aetna has completed its review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider for a non-preferred prescription medication, received at our office on April 6, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated April 8, 2026.

The requested medication is classified as non-preferred under your plan's formulary. Based on Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB) criteria and your plan's pharmacy benefit, coverage of a non-preferred agent requires that a preferred formulary alternative first be tried. Our records do not establish that a preferred lower-tier alternative has been tried, and a preferred agent remains available at a lower cost share. Accordingly, the requested medication is denied at this time.

This determination also reflects a plan exclusion: your benefit plan excludes coverage of non-preferred specialty agents where a formulary alternative has not been exhausted, and that exclusion applies to the requested service.

Our coverage criteria for this drug class reflect efficacy data characterized in the ages 12 to 17 population; on that basis the preferred formulary alternative is considered first-line, and the requested non-preferred agent is not supported for authorization at this time.

You or your provider may request a copy of the claim file and the specific clinical criteria applied to this determination at no charge by contacting Member Services at the number on your ID card. A peer-to-peer discussion with an Aetna medical director may be requested.

If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to file an internal appeal within 180 days of the date of this letter, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization. Appeals may be submitted in writing to Aetna Appeals, P.O. Box 14463, Lexington, KY 40512, or by calling the number on your ID card.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Pharmacy Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 47-year-old woman with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (G35) diagnosed in 2016, covered under a fully-insured employer-sponsored plan issued in her state of residence. Over the past two years she experienced two clinical relapses and new T2 and gadolinium-enhancing lesions on MRI while fully adherent to dimethyl fumarate 240 mg twice daily, the plan's preferred first-line oral agent, which she tolerated but to which she demonstrated inadequate disease control. Her treating neurologist escalated therapy and submitted a prior authorization for ozanimod (Zeposia), an FDA-approved oral sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulator indicated for relapsing forms of MS in adults. The submission documented the prior failure of the preferred agent, baseline ECG and ophthalmologic clearance, and a current EDSS of 2.5. Ozanimod's pivotal efficacy and its FDA approval are established in adults, and adult MS disease-modifying therapy is governed by the 2018 American Academy of Neurology guideline; criteria characterized in the ages 12 to 17 population do not apply to this 47-year-old adult. Her state's step-therapy override statute requires a formulary exception when the preferred agent has already been tried and failed, a circumstance the record clearly establishes.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches the assigned cell field-for-field; G35 is neurology; ozanimod is a realistic RRMS DMT; rationale instantiates formulary_tier_dispute.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Aetna non-preferred oral DMT step-therapy denial is a very common real PA fight; ignored prior failure + misapplied age-band criteria are authentic exploitable gaps.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Aetna CPB citation, Lexington KY appeals address, Aetna Pharmacy Management sign-off, cool procedural cadence; no cross-insurer tells.

denial_logic.reasoning: Circular non-preferred-requires-preferred-trial logic with vague CPB reference and a stacked plan exclusion; not airtight, fits formulary_tier_dispute.

clinical_realism.reasoning: Correct DMF 240mg BID dose, breakthrough with new enhancing lesions, ozanimod S1P modulator with correct ECG/ophthalmologic workup, EDSS 2.5.

diagnosis_treatment_match.reasoning: Ozanimod is FDA-approved oral DMT for relapsing MS; clean match to RRMS.

diversity_delta.reasoning: No neighbour shares MS, an oral S1P DMT, or this trajectory; only the formulary_tier_dispute tag overlaps (a carpal-tunnel case), clinical content unique.

diversity_statistical.reasoning: Max trigram-Jaccard: corpus=0.149 (nearest=case_101_cigna_mednec), neighbours=0.0. Thresholds: warn>=0.35, fail>=0.5.

safety_redactor.reasoning: No banned topics; adult patient; in-scope fully-insured employer-sponsored commercial plan.

contradiction_hunter.reasoning: Gender, diagnosis, drug, dates stable across documents; the ages-12-17 evidence claim is an appealable error the record rebuts, not an unstable scenario fact.

llm_tell_detector.reasoning: Authentic dry insurer template and dense clinical detail; no GPT tells, listicles, or symmetric rhythm.

overall_tone.reasoning: Cold procedural insurer register, realistic vagueness, no apology; plain neutral clinical narrative.

financial_auditor.reasoning: Only qualitative 'lower cost share' language; no exact out-of-pocket figure; no Medicare/Medicaid conflation.

legal_auditor.reasoning: Plausible CPB framing, concrete 180-day window, external IRO rights, ERISA specifics omitted; no invented statutes; age-band issue is clinical not legal.

demographic_validator.reasoning: 47-year-old woman with RRMS is epidemiologically apt; consistent female pronouns; ages-12-17 line is a payer-policy defect, not a profile mismatch.

scope_guard.reasoning: Aetna in-scope, fully-insured employer-sponsored commercial, adult 47, no live-filing/representation language.

date_sanity.reasoning: Received Apr 6 / determined Apr 8 2026 in correct order; 180-day window valid; dx 2016 and 2-year relapse history stack plausibly.

citation_traceability.reasoning: Generic Aetna CPB reference with no fabricated bulletin code/edition; realistically vague; invites criteria-on-request.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Factually false step-therapy premise (preferred agent already failed), pediatric age-band criteria applied to an adult, and a state step-therapy override mandate the record satisfies. Easy appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Denial's core premise is factually false: it claims no preferred agent was tried, but the record documents adherent failure of dimethyl fumarate with relapses and new MRI lesions
Wrong/inapplicable clinical criteria cited: pediatric (ages 12-17) efficacy data applied to a 47-year-old adult, where ozanimod is FDA-approved in adults under the 2018 AAN guideline
State step-therapy override statute mandates a formulary exception when the preferred agent has failed; the plan is fully-insured so the mandate preempts the cited plan exclusion
strong_defenses: Procedurally sound letter: proper 180-day internal appeal window, external IRO review rights, peer-to-peer option, and free access to claim file and clinical criteria
